TextENGNew: Mion and the others had to help set up for the Watanagashi festival, and they invited me along, but I refused.
...Of course I did.
How could they be leisurely preparing for some festival at a time like this?@kI questioned Mion's priorities.
The first thing I did when I got home was take a shower.
Not to wash off sweat.@k...Because I needed to be even colder, even calmer.
As I already understood, killing that man, in and of itself, would be absurdly easy.
...The truly difficult part would be ensuring that I wasn't arrested afterward—that we could reclaim our peaceful everyday life.
When I thought about it, that wasn't easy at all.
...Japan's police boasted the highest clearance rate in the world.
To deceive them and pull off the perfect crime... would be no simple feat.
...But it wasn't 100%.
Even the world's finest police force couldn't achieve a 100% clearance rate.
...You often saw articles about wrongful conviction trials in the news.
...The courts were debating whether the charges were false—but as for the real perpetrator, that person was completely off the radar.
In other words... perfect crimes did exist.
No—more than that. A perfect crime was nothing short of a work of art.
One might even say they were revered.
You only needed to look at the flood of mystery novels throughout history.
They dealt with all manner of locked rooms, alibis, and baffling cases—but what they really wanted to showcase was one thing:@k...the beauty of the perfect crime.
In the word 'perfect'—that expression of flawlessness—there dwelled a kind of divinity.@kYes.@kA perfect crime was a feat only a god could achieve.
...Viewed in that light... the serial mysterious deaths they called 'Oyashiro-sama's curse'@kwere indeed, without question, the work of a god.
Each individual case appeared to have been solved, but the incidents kept occurring every year, unstoppable.
...Yes—divine handiwork. Perfect crimes.
And I would be adding my own crime to that god's—Oyashiro-sama's—four consecutive years of perfect crimes.
changeReason: The original says 'setting up my own crime before' the series, but the Japanese 列する means to add/line up alongside — Keiichi is adding his crime to the existing chain, not preceding it.

TextENGNew: ...Put boldly, this was nothing less than a challenge to a god.
Reckless courage was needed to make the decision... but constructing the plan demanded a composure colder than ice.
Kill like flame.
Plan like ice.
I would have to harbor these two contradictions within me from this moment on...
Using mystery novels as a reference—that actually wasn't a bad approach.
Every now and then, a kidnapping employing some novel method would make the news, and after the arrest, it would come out that the perpetrator had been inspired by a particular novel.
changeReason: The original reverses the relationship: it says the crime inspired novels, but the Japanese says the perpetrator was inspired by a novel — the novel came first.

TextENGNew: Mystery novelists spent endless hours, day after day, polishing their ultimate artistic crimes.
...Imitating their work could be a shortcut to devising the perfect crime in a limited timeframe.
Of course, I hadn't read nearly enough mystery novels to brag about it.
...The one in the family who loved mystery novels... was Mom.

She'd read all the classics from famous mystery authors around the world.@kAnd every time she watched a mystery drama on TV, she'd pick it apart mercilessly—'how cheap,' 'the trick is too obvious,' and so on.
changeReason: The new translation omits Keiichi's internal musing '...what would the perfect crime be to her...' (そんなお袋にとって完全犯罪とは), which establishes his mental shift toward planning.

TextENGNew: ``What do you think is the most well-executed perfect crime in all the mystery novels you've read, Mom?``
changeReason: The original uses first person 'What do I think' (as if Mom is speaking to herself), but this is Keiichi's dialogue asking his mother — the new translation correctly uses 'What do you think... Mom?'

TextENGNew: "...Yeah.@k@vS03/01/140100551. You've read a ton of mystery novels, right, Mom?@k@vS03/01/140100552. I figured you might know a lot about that stuff."
"That's what makes reading them fun.@k@vS03/00/142700007. If you know the trick from the start, it takes all the enjoyment out."
...It wasn't like I was asking for entertainment.
...I'd have to flatter Mom somehow to draw the answer out of her, even as she tried to dodge the question...
"Let me think... Well-done mystery novels... hmm..."
"They all have their own unique appeal, so it's hard to single one out, but if I had to choose..."
"...I don't want to know which novels you liked.@k@vS03/01/140100554. I want to know which one you thought had the most perfect crime—one that was truly flawless."
"If it were truly a perfect trick, there'd be no room for the detective to solve it."
...She had a point.@k...But that wasn't helpful.

"Mystery novels are about enjoying the process of solving a solvable puzzle.@k@vS03/00/142700012. An unsolvable puzzle wouldn't make for a story.@k@vS03/00/142700013. There'd be nothing to write about."

``...I see.@k@vS03/01/140100556. ...Yeah, that makes sense.@k@vS03/01/140100557. ...If the trick is too perfect, you can't write about the detective solving it. So they don't write about tricks like that.``
changeReason: The new translation rephrases Keiichi's understanding: the Japanese has him parroting back 'if the protagonist can't solve it, the story doesn't work,' but the new version substitutes a different conclusion about writers avoiding perfect tricks.

TextENGNew: Had I been too simplistic in my thinking?
...A mystery novel was, at the end of the day, a form of entertainment.
changeReason: The Japanese ゲーム小説 specifically means 'game novel' (a novel structured as a puzzle/game for the reader); the new translation's 'form of entertainment' loses this specific genre concept.

TextENGNew: They were written so that the reader could reach the goal, so that the mystery could be solved.
But something that soft wouldn't work for me.
...I needed a trick so absolute, so airtight, that we could return completely to our normal lives.
"Which brings me to a point, Keiichi.@k@vS03/00/142700014. ...Do you understand what that means? A truly perfect crime... can never even become a story."
Mom fixed me with a sharp, knowing look and smiled mischievously.
...I'd never gotten to read it, but apparently she'd once written a mystery novel herself and submitted it to some literary prize.
changeReason: The new translation omits その血が疼いたようだ ('that literary instinct of hers seemed to stir'), which characterizes Mom's enthusiasm about the topic.

TextENGNew: ...But what did she mean by it 'can't even become a story'...?
"Keiichi, what are the essential building blocks of any story?"
``Setup, development, twist, and resolution.``
changeReason: The Japanese 起承転結 is a four-part narrative structure (setup, development, twist, resolution), not the three-part Western 'beginning, middle, end' used in the original.

TextENGNew: "Correct.@k@vS03/00/142700018. The most crucial element, obviously, is the 'setup.'@k@vS03/00/142700019. If there's no 'setup,' then the story can't even begin."
"Well, yeah.@k@vS03/01/140100560. ...If nothing happens, nothing starts."

``Exactly.@k@vS03/00/142700021. If nothing happens, there's no incident.@k@vS03/00/142700022. ...In other words, a truly perfect crime doesn't even have a 'setup.'@k@vS03/00/142700023. That's how perfect it is.``
changeReason: The new translation condenses Mom's step-by-step logical chain (no incident -> no detective -> no mystery -> no solution -> ultimate perfect crime) into fewer steps, losing the methodical deduction that characterizes her reasoning.

TextENGNew: "...Wait... hold on a second.@k@vS03/01/140100562. ...Could you explain that again?"
Mom had just... told me something extraordinary.
changeReason: The original translates とてつもない as 'absurd' (negative/dismissive), but it means 'extraordinary/tremendous' — Keiichi is awed, not skeptical.

TextENGNew: ...She'd said it so casually that I'd almost let it pass... but she had said something—@k...something monumentally important.
"It's not really that complicated.@k@vS03/00/142700026. ...Basically, if the crime is never discovered, that's all there is to it."
"...A crime that... is never discovered in the first place."
"For example... hmm, let's say...@k@vS03/00/142700028. There's an old man living alone, deep in a forest that nobody ever enters. All by himself."
"Okay..."

"One day, the old man is killed with an axe.@k@vS03/00/142700030. ...The culprit, by the way, is... hmm, let's say the old man's son.@k@vS03/00/142700031. He came over to the old man's house and they were drinking together, but they got into a fight and he killed him."
changeReason: Minor stylistic rephrasing; both old and new correctly identify the killer as the old man's son, and the dialogue content is essentially the same.

TextENGNew: ...As a plot, this was incredibly cheap.
An impulsive crime with zero planning.@kAbout as far from a perfect crime as you could get...

``But, Keiichi.@k@vS03/00/142700033. This house is deep, deep in the forest.@k@vS03/00/142700034. Nobody ever goes there.@k@vS03/00/142700035. Nobody even knows about it.@k@vS03/00/142700036. ...Then this incident will never come to light.@k@vS03/00/142700037. Ever.``
changeReason: The new translation replaces the son's cover-up (returning to town and claiming the old man was fine) with generic text about nobody visiting — losing the key element of the criminal actively concealing the crime.

TextENGNew: "...What about that is a perfect crime?"

"Keiichi.@k@vS03/00/142700038. ...This incident would only become 'an incident' once the old man's body was discovered.@k@vS03/00/142700039. But nobody visits the deep forest.@k@vS03/00/142700040. So nobody discovers the body."
...The 'setup' would never arrive...
"So the story never begins.@k@vS03/00/142700041. The crime isn't discovered, so no detective is called in.@k@vS03/00/142700042. Nobody's suspicious in the first place, so no mystery even arises."

``...Everyone would just assume the old man was still living by himself, deep in the forest...@k@vS03/00/142700044. And then the old man's face floats up in the sky, and that's The End.@k@vS03/00/142700045. ...How's that, something like this?``
changeReason: The new translation loses Mom's storytelling flourish — the Japanese has her describe the old man's face appearing in the sky followed by 'The End' (a TV drama-style ending), replaced by generic commentary about nobody noticing.

TextENGNew: "...Th-... That's amazing...!!@k@vS03/01/140100567. That's a legitimate trick, isn't it?! It's a genuine perfect crime!!"

``But it wouldn't make for an entertaining novel.@k@vS03/00/142700047. Mystery novels are, at the end of the day, entertainment.@k@vS03/00/142700048. A story that can never begin is a story that can never be written.``
changeReason: The original faithfully translates 完全犯罪の指南書にはなり得ない ('they can't serve as instruction manuals for perfect crimes'), but the new translation substitutes a different philosophical statement about stories.

TextENGNew: ...I'd been keeping the conversation light to avoid arousing Mom's suspicion... but inside, I was electrified.
changeReason: Both describe keeping conversation light while internally excited; 'fool her' vs 'avoid suspicion' vs JPN 'tease' are all approximate.

TextENGNew: Because I now understood: a perfect crime required no special tools, no bizarre terrain, no mysterious new drug, no elaborate scheme funded with vast sums of money.
What mattered was... the absence of a 'setup.'@k...As long as the crime was never discovered... that was all it took.
To put it simply:@kif I could kill him without anyone finding out,@kand dispose of the body without anyone finding out,@k...that would essentially be a perfect crime.
...Of course, if someone simply vanished one day, it would raise suspicion.
...But that didn't apply to this man.
Last year, when the aunt died, he had fled the village in terror of Oyashiro-sama's curse.
When the night of Watanagashi came around again, everyone would simply assume he'd gotten scared and run off once more.
The man had been drifting around Okinomiya anyway, no fixed address, no steady job.
changeReason: The original adds 'living with a lover' which has no basis in the Japanese; the source text describes the man as jobless, aimless, and with no fixed address in Okinomiya.

TextENGNew: If he vanished on a whim, no one would bat an eye.
He was universally despised.@k...If he disappeared, who would care where he went...?!
The more I thought about it, the clearer it became: killing this man was remarkably easy.
There was nothing more to be gained from thinking at home.
I slipped out of the house and pulled my bicycle from the garage.
...For now, it didn't matter where I was headed.
I just wanted to ride through the wind, let the cold air wash over me, and cool the mounting excitement in my blood.
...The killing location. The method. The timing. The disposal of the body.
...If I could pin down just those four things... the plan would be complete.
The fact that there was so little to decide sent a shiver through me.
...I'd been bracing myself for a painstaking process of assembling some enormous, elaborate plan...@k...and yet all that remained was to sort out these few details.
Today was Saturday.
...If I was going to kill him on the night of Watanagashi—carrying out Oyashiro-sama's curse in my own name—I had only 24 hours left.
...Satoko.@k...Just hold on... for 24 more hours.
I can't even imagine what further torment you're enduring... but just a little longer... please hold on.
I know you can't bear even one more second of it... I know that...
But please... just hang on...@k...Your Nii-nii... will save you. I promise...
Outside, the cicadas were still in full chorus.
...Early afternoon.@k...Evening was still a long way off.
...I glanced up at the sky—at some point, it had turned overcast.
Come to think of it, as I left the house, I'd caught a snippet of the weather forecast on TV saying it might rain on Sunday...
The sky was the color of lead.
...If even a rumble of thunder sounded, a sudden downpour could hit at any moment.
...My first destination was the school.
Before explaining why I was heading there, I need to explain what method I had chosen to kill the man.
...The most fitting death I could bestow upon him, I decided, was@k...yes, the same as last year's curse.@k...The same fate his wife had received.@kBludgeoning.
Wouldn't bludgeoning be somewhat unreliable, though...?
You might think that stabbing with a blade would be more certain...
But this, of course, was a choice I'd made after careful deliberation.
...Just think about it realistically.
Blades beyond a certain length—ten-something centimeters, I think—were prohibited by law.
The longest blade I could get my hands on would still be shorter than a ruler.
...Against a potentially resisting opponent, you can probably imagine how inadequate such limited reach would be.
A staff-like weapon with greater reach would be far more advantageous.
It might lack inherent lethality, but since I could simply keep swinging until the job was done, the end result would be the same.
So: a staff-like weapon with devastating power and excellent portability. What fits that description?
...By this point, the answer should be obvious: a metal baseball bat.
I don't need to explain... just how terrifying a weapon a metal bat can be.
And what's particularly noteworthy is that it blends seamlessly into everyday life—carrying one wouldn't raise the slightest suspicion.
...Those two points alone would have been sufficient reason to choose a metal bat as the weapon...@kbut there was actually a third reason I wanted a specific metal bat.
Saturday afternoon—classmates were still running around the schoolyard.
If I showed up among them, it would be a perfectly normal sight. No one would think twice.
...Obtaining the weapon without arousing suspicion was, in fact, another element I couldn't afford to overlook.
...If someone like me, who never played baseball, walked into a sporting goods store and asked for a metal bat... it would obviously look suspicious.
...To prevent the 'setup' from ever occurring... I needed to exercise extreme caution at every step.
That was why: the school.
...A location where my presence wouldn't be suspicious... where I could obtain the weapon.
I peered through the window into the classroom... and as expected, no one was inside.
The only students who would linger in the classroom after school were our club members during club time.
...Without us, the after-school classroom was nothing but an empty room.
...Without glancing around nervously... as if I'd simply come back for something I'd forgotten... I casually entered through the shoe entrance.
...Sensei appeared to be absorbed in paperwork in the teachers' lounge.
She wasn't in the hallway.
...Naturally, yet swift as a shadow... I entered the classroom.
The empty classroom was filled with a strange, stagnant atmosphere—one you could never sense when it was full of people.
...An empty, deserted classroom.
...Maybe while no one was watching... the desks crept around on their own, licking the stains on the floor with wet, rasping tongues.
changeReason: The original says 'licking the floors clean of dirt,' but the Japanese 染み means 'stains' — the desks are licking at specific stains, a more unsettling image than general cleaning.

TextENGNew: ...If I barged in on them suddenly...@kwould the startled desks lunge at me, crunch my bones to pieces, and swallow me whole?
I was annoyed at myself for squandering valuable thinking time on a pointless fantasy.
...The students' lockers lined the back of the classroom.
The locker I was looking for... wasn't my own.@k...It was one I'd stumbled upon by chance, one day... a forbidden locker.
"...There it is... Houjou.@k@vS03/01/140100570. ...No mistake."
...Everyone got one locker, so why did only Satoko seem to have two?@kThat question had come up once.
...It turned out to be the locker of Satoshi—another Houjou.
...Of course, even if the locker belonged to an absent student... looking inside was a shameless thing to do.
...But once... I'd lost to my curiosity and peeked inside...
What was in there had been mundane and unremarkable... I'd felt no interest at the time, and until this very moment, I'd forgotten all about it.
...But now that my mind had sharpened to a degree that deserved the word 'awakened'...@k...I remembered. Buried in those old memories... it was there.
Creeeak...
I opened the locker... and a stench like a filthy sports storage room—mold and stale sweat intermingling—washed over me.
...Grimacing at the smell... I searched for it.
Yes.
...Satoshi had been on Coach's little league baseball team.
...Inside the locker was a Hinamizawa Fighters uniform... and it.
...The one Satoshi had used... a metal bat...
Yes.@k...This was the reason I'd wanted this particular bat.
...This murder would be carried out by my hands... but in truth, it wasn't my role—it was Satoshi's.
...But I, who called myself Nii-nii in his stead, would carry it out for him.
...One of the rules... for acting as his proxy.
I gripped the metal bat... tightly.
...It was light.
...And yet... its tip held enough weight that... if you swung it down with all your might, the result would be horrifying—that much was easy to imagine.
...Satoshi.@k...I'm giving you one last chance to save your sister.
...Lend me your strength.
...I'll do what you were too cowardly to do.
...So if there's even a shred of love for your sister left in you... lend me... your strength.
There is no weapon more fitting to deliver justice to that man than your bat.
Now... I just needed to hide it somewhere on the school grounds.
...Tomorrow, right before the act, I'd swing by the school to retrieve it.
...I couldn't ignore the possibility that someone might see me carrying it home and find it suspicious.
Leaving it here at the school eliminated that concern.
Besides, tomorrow was Sunday.@kAnd not just any Sunday... it was the village's biggest festival.
...No one would be coming to school.
I exited through the shoe entrance... and approached a piece of heavy construction equipment parked nearby.
...Our teacher had strictly warned us never to touch it, so none of the children ever went near it.
...They wouldn't even go close, for fear of being found out and teased about it.
changeReason: The original says students would 'be in huge trouble,' but the Japanese はやし立てる means they'd be 'teased/mocked' by peers — social pressure from classmates, not punishment from authority.

TextENGNew: In the shadow of that machine... I gently hid the bat.
...The odds of anyone moving this machine tomorrow were essentially zero.
...Because tomorrow was Sunday.
Government workers had the day off.
...And from what my classmates said, this machine had been abandoned here for years.@k...Something left unmoved for that long wasn't going to suddenly be used tomorrow.
...The sunlight was scorchingly hot. For a moment, my head went hazy.
Had the sun... been this punishing the whole time...?
A moment of dulled thoughts like this... was a moment when you let your guard down.@k...I tapped my head twice, then carefully surveyed my surroundings.
...Good.@k...........Next... the disposal of the body.
